claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'm inquiring about. His name is Alex and he's 19 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 19 years old, I need to ask some important questions about his situation.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training? (For example, is he in full-time education, such as A-levels, further education college, or approved training?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is in education. He's studying for his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful, thank you. And do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Is Alex currently working, and if so, is he working 24 hours or more per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does work. He has a part-time job at a local shop, and actually it is quite a lot of hours - he works full-time alongside his studies.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Just to clarify - when you say he works "full-time," does that mean he's working 24 hours or more per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - he works 24 hours or more per week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit.

Based on what you've told me, unfortunately Alex would not be eligible for Child Benefit because he is working 24 hours or more per week, even though he is still in approved education (studying for his A-levels).

According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. While Alex is still in approved education, the fact that he's working 24 hours or more per week means Child Benefit would stop.

Let me provide you with the formal assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry that the outcome isn't what you might have hoped for. If Alex's working hours were to reduce to below 24 hours per week in the future while he continues his education, you may become eligible for Child Benefit at that point.

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and is in approved education (studying for A-levels), which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue until age 20. However, Alex is working 24 hours or more per week. According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex is working 24 hours or more per week, this disqualifies him from Child Benefit eligibility, despite still being in education.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for one child, Alex, who is 19 years old. While Alex is in approved education (A-levels), which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue, he is working 24 hours or more per week. Under Child Benefit rules, working 24+ hours per week disqualifies a child from eligibility, making the claimant ineligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 19 years old and is in approved education (studying for A-levels), which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue until age 20. However, Alex is working 24 hours or more per week. According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex is working 24 hours or more per week, this disqualifies him from Child Benefit eligibility, despite still being in education.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for one child, Alex, who is 19 years old. While Alex is in approved education (A-levels), which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue, he is working 24 hours or more per week. Under Child Benefit rules, working 24+ hours per week disqualifies a child from eligibility, making the claimant ineligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
